intro: Hyperswitch and Yuno both show up under the payments orchestration category, but they solve slightly different jobs. Hyperswitch uses a free model, while Yuno runs on pricing that starts at $79. Both sit at low lock-in. Transparency lands at 5/5 versus 2/5. Hyperswitch fits teams working on open-source payments switch routing to multiple processors via unified API, while Yuno is a closer match when the job is Multi-PSP routing for higher conversion. Worth noting: Hyperswitch is explicitly not for small teams without DevOps capacity or those needing a fully managed, SaaS-only product; Yuno is explicitly not for single-market startups. The honest trade-off: Hyperswitch trades off on self-hosting demands serious infra maturity; Yuno trades off on single-market startups. On the plus side, Hyperswitch highlights open source under Apache 2.0 — fully self-hostable, while Yuno points to Multi-PSP routing for higher conversion. Hyperswitch's documentation also calls out written in Rust for throughput and low latency. Yuno similarly notes enterprise with multiple payment providers.
quick_take: Hyperswitch is for open-source payments switch routing to multiple; Yuno is for Multi-PSP routing for higher conversion; decide on pricing model.
verdict_a: Choose Hyperswitch if your project is open-source payments switch routing to multiple processors via unified API, you want to keep future migration cheap, strong SDKs and docs (5/5) are a priority.
verdict_b: Choose Yuno if your project is Multi-PSP routing for higher conversion, you want to keep future migration cheap, you can tolerate Yuno's limited public transparency (2/5).

faq.a: Hyperswitch uses a free model, and Yuno uses pricing that starts at $79. The pricing models are different, so a direct cheaper-than comparison depends on your volume and usage pattern. Hyperswitch offers a free tier; Yuno does not.

faq.q: Can I migrate from Hyperswitch to Yuno?
faq.a: Our data puts Hyperswitch at low lock-in (open-source payments switch), and Yuno at low lock-in (orchestration abstraction helps migration). Moving from Hyperswitch to Yuno should be manageable, though you'll still need to replay integrations and re-test flows end-to-end.

Hyperswitch scores 5/5 on developer experience in our data, while Yuno scores 4/5, so Hyperswitch has the edge on docs and SDK quality by that measure. That gap is reinforced by transparency scores of 5/5 versus 2/5. Still, run a small integration spike on both before deciding — team familiarity with a given SDK style often matters more than a one-point score gap.

faq.q: Is Yuno a good alternative to Hyperswitch?
faq.a: Yuno is a reasonable alternative to Hyperswitch when your workload leans more toward enterprise than engineering-led teams wanting full control of an orchestrator without paying per-transaction SaaS fees. The pricing model shifts too — Hyperswitch is a free model, Yuno is pricing that starts at $79 — so expect the cost profile to change as well. One caveat: Yuno is explicitly not for single-market startups, so check that constraint against your use-case before switching.
